name: Chesapeake Bay
description:
The Virginia portion of the Chesapeake Bay is one of the most productive estuaries in the world, stretching from the mouth of the bay at Virginia Beach north to the Potomac River. The southern Chesapeake is the epicenter of East Coast striped bass fishing, with trophy rockfish exceeding 50 pounds caught each winter and spring in the bay's main shipping channels and around the Chesapeake Bay Bridge-Tunnel. Red drum have become an increasingly exciting fishery in recent years, with bull reds over 40 pounds taken along the Eastern Shore barrier islands and lower bay flats.

With over 7,000 miles of tidal shoreline, Virginia's Chesapeake Bay offers virtually limitless fishing access through hundreds of public boat ramps, marinas, and fishing piers. The bay's legendary blue crab population supports both recreational crabbing and a commercial industry that defines the region's culture and cuisine. Flounder, speckled trout, and croaker provide consistent action in the shallower bays and creek mouths, while bluefish blitz the surface during summer and fall. From the urban waterfront of Norfolk to the remote marshes of the Eastern Shore, the Virginia Chesapeake offers a saltwater fishing experience matched by few places on earth.

name: Smith Mountain Lake
description:
Smith Mountain Lake is Virginia's premier freshwater fishing destination, a 20,600-acre reservoir nestled in the Blue Ridge foothills southwest of Lynchburg. The lake is famous for its trophy striped bass fishery, with landlocked stripers regularly exceeding 30 pounds and fish over 40 pounds caught each year. Largemouth bass fishing is equally impressive, with the lake's 500 miles of developed and undeveloped shoreline providing diverse habitat from shallow coves to deep rocky points.

Smith Mountain Lake State Park and numerous public boat ramps provide excellent access to this sprawling reservoir. The lake's musky population has grown into a legitimate trophy fishery, with fish over 50 inches documented in recent years. Crappie fishing is outstanding in the spring around standing timber and brush piles in the lake's many coves and creek arms. The thriving community around Smith Mountain Lake offers full-service marinas, waterfront restaurants, guide services, and vacation rentals that make it an ideal destination for multi-day fishing trips in the Virginia mountains.

name: Lake Anna
description:
Lake Anna is a 13,000-acre reservoir in central Virginia that offers a unique fishing experience due to its warm-water discharge from the North Anna Nuclear Power Station. The lake is divided into a public side and a warm side, with the heated water on the warm side extending the growing season and creating year-round fishing opportunities that are rare at this latitude. Largemouth bass grow exceptionally fast in the warm side and reach trophy sizes, while the public side offers more traditional seasonal fishing patterns.

Lake Anna State Park and several public boat ramps provide access to both sides of the reservoir. Striped bass fishing is excellent in the deeper channels and around the discharge area, where warm water attracts baitfish and predators throughout the winter months. Crappie and bluegill are abundant in the standing timber and brush piles that dot the lake's many coves, providing outstanding panfish opportunities for families. The lake's location about 60 miles from Richmond and 90 miles from Washington, D.C., makes it a popular weekend fishing destination for anglers from both metro areas.

name: Kerr Lake
description:
Kerr Lake, also known as Buggs Island Lake, is a 50,000-acre reservoir on the Virginia-North Carolina border that ranks among the top bass fishing lakes in the mid-Atlantic region. The lake's largemouth bass fishery is outstanding, with consistent catches of quality fish in the 3- to 5-pound range and trophy bass exceeding 10 pounds taken each year. Striped bass and hybrid striped bass provide exciting open-water action, with fish chasing schools of herring across the lake's vast surface.

The Army Corps of Engineers manages several recreation areas and public boat ramps around Kerr Lake's 800 miles of shoreline, and the town of Clarksville serves as the primary hub for visiting anglers. Crappie fishing is legendary at Kerr Lake, with spring producing incredible catches of slab crappie around the lake's abundant standing timber and brush structure. The lake's southern location on the Virginia-North Carolina border provides a longer growing season than more northern waters, and catfish grow to impressive sizes in the warm, fertile waters. Major bass tournament circuits frequently visit Kerr Lake, cementing its reputation as a premier competitive fishing venue.

name: Claytor Lake
description:
Claytor Lake is a 4,475-acre reservoir in the New River Valley of southwestern Virginia, offering outstanding multi-species fishing in a scenic mountain setting. The lake supports an impressive diversity of gamefish, including both largemouth and smallmouth bass, striped bass, musky, walleye, and crappie. The musky fishery is particularly noteworthy, with the New River strain of muskellunge producing fish over 40 inches in the lake's deeper channels and around major creek inflows.

Claytor Lake State Park provides a well-maintained boat ramp and marina, along with camping, swimming, and hiking facilities. The lake's position on the upper New River system means it receives clean, cool water that supports both warm-water and cool-water species. Smallmouth bass provide excellent catch-and-release fishing along the rocky banks and ledges, while striped bass are caught by trolling live bait and large lures over the main channel. The New River Valley's charming small towns and proximity to Virginia Tech in Blacksburg add to the appeal of Claytor Lake as a fishing and vacation destination.

name: James River
description:
The James River is Virginia's signature waterway, flowing 348 miles from the Appalachian Mountains through Richmond to the Chesapeake Bay, and it supports one of the finest smallmouth bass fisheries in the eastern United States. The stretch from Lynchburg through Scottsville to Richmond is world-class smallmouth water, with bronze beauties averaging 14 to 18 inches found in the river's riffles, ledges, and deep pools. The Richmond fall line section offers the unique experience of catching smallmouth bass, largemouth bass, and shad within sight of the state capitol skyline.

Public boat ramps are located throughout the James River corridor, with the Virginia Department of Wildlife Resources and local municipalities maintaining access points from the mountain headwaters to the tidal reaches near Williamsburg. Musky inhabit the upper James and have produced fish over 50 inches, establishing this section as a legitimate trophy musky destination. Flathead catfish have colonized the lower river and provide trophy fishing opportunities, with fish exceeding 60 pounds documented. The spring shad run brings millions of American and hickory shad upriver through Richmond, and striped bass follow the shad migration from the Chesapeake Bay, creating an incredible springtime convergence of fishing opportunities.
